title: Listening - Part 3: Receiving Useful Advice
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about some advice they received. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H why each speaker found the advice helpful. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about some advice they received. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H why each speaker found the advice helpful. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about advice that proved useful.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: In my first term at university, I accepted every invitation and then tried to complete assignments the night before they were due. I became exhausted and assumed I simply wasn't clever enough for the course. My tutor listened and suggested a weekly timetable. [19]Once I started breaking each assignment into smaller tasks and giving each one a particular day, the work stopped feeling impossible and I submitted everything on time.[/19] It sounds obvious, but I needed somebody to show me how to approach it.||Speaker 2 Man: I had decided to study business because several relatives said it would lead to secure work. Before applying, I talked to a careers adviser and mentioned how much time I spend creating illustrations. [20]She advised me to investigate design courses rather than ignore the subject I genuinely enjoyed, and after attending an open day I changed my application completely.[/20] I'm now on a design course and working harder than ever, but it feels like the right kind of hard work for me.||Speaker 3 Woman: I was about to take my driving test and felt so nervous that I considered cancelling it. I kept imagining making one mistake and failing immediately. My older cousin had taken the test recently. [21]She explained that the examiner did not expect perfection and that one small error need not mean failure, which stopped me treating every moment as a disaster before it had happened.[/21] I still felt nervous on the day, but it was manageable, and I passed on my first attempt.||Speaker 4 Man: My flatmate and I argued because she often had friends round late at night while I had early morning classes. I became increasingly annoyed, but avoided saying anything clearly, so the atmosphere just got worse. [22]A friend advised me to discuss specific times calmly and suggest one or two evenings when visitors would be fine, instead of accusing my flatmate of being inconsiderate.[/22] We spoke the next day and agreed on a routine. I wish I'd dealt with it weeks earlier.||Speaker 5 Woman: When I moved into my first flat, I wanted to buy all the furniture immediately. I was about to order a new sofa and dining table using most of my savings. My brother told me to wait. [23]He suggested searching local second-hand listings first, and I found excellent furniture for less than a third of what the new pieces would have cost.[/23] I used the remaining money for boring but necessary things such as curtains and kitchen equipment, which I hadn't even considered.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: In my first term at university, I accepted every invitation and then tried to complete assignments the night before they were due. I became exhausted and assumed I simply wasn't clever enough for the course. My tutor listened and suggested a weekly timetable. Once I started breaking each assignment into smaller tasks and giving each one a particular day, the work stopped feeling impossible and I submitted everything on time. It sounds obvious, but I needed somebody to show me how to approach it.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: I had decided to study business because several relatives said it would lead to secure work. Before applying, I talked to a careers adviser and mentioned how much time I spend creating illustrations. She advised me to investigate design courses rather than ignore the subject I genuinely enjoyed, and after attending an open day I changed my application completely. I'm now on a design course and working harder than ever, but it feels like the right kind of hard work for me.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: I was about to take my driving test and felt so nervous that I considered cancelling it. I kept imagining making one mistake and failing immediately. My older cousin had taken the test recently. She explained that the examiner did not expect perfection and that one small error need not mean failure, which stopped me treating every moment as a disaster before it had happened. I still felt nervous on the day, but it was manageable, and I passed on my first attempt.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: My flatmate and I argued because she often had friends round late at night while I had early morning classes. I became increasingly annoyed, but avoided saying anything clearly, so the atmosphere just got worse. A friend advised me to discuss specific times calmly and suggest one or two evenings when visitors would be fine, instead of accusing my flatmate of being inconsiderate. We spoke the next day and agreed on a routine. I wish I'd dealt with it weeks earlier.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: When I moved into my first flat, I wanted to buy all the furniture immediately. I was about to order a new sofa and dining table using most of my savings. My brother told me to wait. He suggested searching local second-hand listings first, and I found excellent furniture for less than a third of what the new pieces would have cost. I used the remaining money for boring but necessary things such as curtains and kitchen equipment, which I hadn't even considered.

options.text: It encouraged them to change a plan.
It made them feel less anxious.
It helped them organise their workload.
It enabled them to deal with a disagreement.
It showed them how to make new friends.
It helped them solve a technical problem.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The tutor's planning suggestion helped the speaker handle academic work and meet deadlines.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The adviser led the speaker to replace a business plan with a design course application.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The cousin's explanation reduced the speaker's anxiety before a driving test.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The advice showed the speaker how to discuss a conflict constructively with a flatmate.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The brother's suggestion allowed the speaker to furnish a flat at a much lower cost.
